text:
AnOlympicsbroadcasting chief has urged camera operators to avoid “sexist” filming of female athletes. The Paris 2024 Olympics have already made ground-breaking changes to reach gender parity - and now its official broadcaster is tackling “unconscious biases” when covering women’s sports.

With the Games having got underway on Friday, head of Olympic Broadcasting Services (OBS) Yiannis Exarchos has been vocal about men's and women’s sports being filmed in the same way. OBS is responsible for the TV coverage of the Olympics, with its images shared with rights holders around the world.

Exarchos revealed that the organisation has already updated its guidelines for camera operators, most of whom are men. And he wants to avoid both stereotypes and sexism from creeping into the coverage over the next two weeks.

“Unfortunately, in some events they [women] are still being filmed in a way that you can identify that stereotypes and sexism remains, even from the way in which some camera operators are framing differently men and women athletes,” the OBS chief executive told reporters in Paris on Sunday.

“Women athletes are not there because they are more attractive or sexy or whatever. They are there because they are elite athletes.”

Exarchos explained how the problem was primarily down to “unconscious bias”, which meant camera operators and TV editors tend to show more close-up shots of women than of men.



Discovery+is the new home of the Olympics. The streamer is set to broadcast 3,800 hours of sport live from Paris.

Sports fans can getDiscovery+ for £3.99a month until the end of 2024 via Amazon Prime's video channels. This also comes with anAmazon Prime 30-day free trial.

He went on to address the scheduling changes implemented for the Paris Games in order to boost women’s sport. That involves allowing women’s competitions to take place after their male counterparts, as will be the case when the women’s marathon becomes the final event of the entire Games.

We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and to improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and 3rd parties based on our understanding. You can unsubscribe at any time. Read ourPrivacy Policy

“The schedules of sporting events have traditionally been biased towards highlighting men’s events,” Exarchos added. “Traditionally, in team sports, you have first women’s finals, and then the men’s final … In strength and combat sports, traditionally you have women’s competitions in the morning and men’s competitions in the afternoon.”

Marie Sallois, who is in charge of gender equality at the International Olympic Committee, claimed that the Paris Games were "de facto the world's largest platform to promote gender equality in and through sport". She highlighted “symbolic moments” in Friday’s opening ceremony, where almost all delegations had a male and female flag carrier and the artistic show paid tribute to influential women in French history.

title: Olympics camera men told to avoid being sexist filming female athletes | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Kasra Moradi
description: The Head of Olympic Broadcasting Services says female Olympians are filmed in a different way to men due to ‘unconscious bias', resulting in more close-up shots
